entries.tutorialTitle: The artificial swarm method
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Splitting a colony before it splits itself.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A colony building swarm cells in late spring is ten days from leaving. The artificial swarm method uses that impulse to expand the apiary rather than lose the bees. The technique moves the flying bees onto the original site with a new queen cell and gives the queenright colony a fresh start on empty comb.

entries.tutorialTitle: Bee stings: minimising and managing
entries.tutorialSubtitle: What reduces sting risk and what to do when it happens.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Getting stung is part of beekeeping, but experienced beekeepers get stung far less than beginners. Most stings happen for predictable reasons: wrong weather, slow movements, wrong clothing, or disturbing the colony near the entrance. Understanding the patterns cuts the frequency; knowing what to do next handles the rest.

entries.tutorialTitle: Breaking a broody hen
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Getting a hen back into lay when she refuses to move.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A broody hen stops laying, monopolises a nest box, pecks at anyone who approaches, and can lose condition if left sitting for weeks on no eggs. Breaking broodiness early protects her health and gets her back into production. The method is straightforward and takes three to five days.

entries.tutorialTitle: Buying a nucleus hive: what to check before you collect
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The questions to ask and the inspection that matters.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A nucleus colony is the standard starting point for a new beekeeper. Most nucs are healthy and well-prepared, but a few basics protect you from buying disease, a failing queen, or a colony that barely has the numbers to build up through summer. Knowing what to look for before you load the nuc into the car makes all the difference.

entries.tutorialTitle: Fitting electric poultry netting
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Fox-proofing a free-range flock with a single roll of netting.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Electric poultry netting is the most practical fox deterrent for a small free-range flock. A 50-metre roll encloses a decent run, sets up in 20 minutes once you know the system, and moves when the grass needs a rest. Getting the energiser matched to the run and the earth spike correctly placed is the difference between a fence that works and one that does not.

entries.tutorialTitle: Worming goats and running a faecal egg count
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Targeted treatment instead of routine dosing.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Goats are more susceptible to gastrointestinal worms than sheep and metabolise wormers differently, making them harder to dose and faster to develop resistance. A faecal egg count before treatment tells you which animals actually need worming and how effective the treatment was. Routine dosing without egg count data is the fastest route to wormer resistance.

entries.tutorialTitle: Grazing rotation planning for a small flock
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Dividing land to prevent poaching, parasite build-up, and pasture degradation.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Keeping the same animals on the same patch of ground continuously degrades the pasture, builds worm burdens, and produces poached muddy ground that is hard to recover. Rotating two or three paddocks (even on a small site) breaks the parasite cycle, allows grass recovery, and reduces the labour of managing a muddy field every winter.

entries.tutorialTitle: Bottling and labelling honey
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Getting the harvest into jars cleanly.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Raw honey extracted from the super needs time to settle before it goes into jars. Rush the bottling and you get foam-topped jars and air pockets. Allow the right settling window, skim once, bottle at the correct temperature, and the result is clear, correctly labelled honey that keeps indefinitely.

entries.tutorialTitle: Keeping a livestock health diary
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The observation log that makes patterns visible over time.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A health diary is not the same as the statutory medicine record. It is the observation notebook where you note anything unusual: a ewe with a cough, a pig that fed slowly, a hen that did not come out in the morning. Over months it reveals patterns that a stock memory alone cannot hold and gives any vet far more useful information than 'she seemed fine until yesterday'.

entries.tutorialTitle: Lamb colostrum management
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The first six hours are the ones that matter most.

entries.tutorialTitle: Teeth clipping in newborn piglets
entries.tutorialSubtitle: When to clip, when to leave, and how to do it without causing harm.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Newborn piglets have eight needle teeth (wolf teeth) that can scratch the sow's teats and cause injuries when littermates compete for the udder. Clipping is the routine management decision in larger commercial systems, but it is a painful procedure and welfare codes advise against routine clipping without a clinical reason. Most smallholders with outdoor-reared sows and small litters do not need to clip.

entries.tutorialTitle: Coccidiosis in kits: recognition and treatment
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The protozoan parasite that kills young rabbits between weeks three and six.

Recognising and treating early saves a litter.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Distinguishing ear mites from otitis media before both progress.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A rabbit shaking its head and scratching at one ear has either ear mites or an ear canal infection (otitis externa), or both. A rabbit with a head tilt has an infection of the middle ear (otitis media). The two conditions look different, need different treatments, and carry very different prognoses. Distinguishing them early determines the outcome.

entries.tutorialTitle: Pasteurellosis in rabbits: managing a chronic colony problem
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The bacterium behind snuffles, abscesses, and sudden deaths.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Pasteurella multocida is present in most rabbit colonies and causes no problems until stress, immune suppression, or a dense population tips susceptible animals into disease. Snuffles (nasal discharge), jaw abscesses, and acute septicaemia are all expressions of the same organism. Management focuses on reducing stress and removing clinical cases rather than eliminating the bacterium entirely.

entries.tutorialTitle: Oxalic acid treatment for varroa in winter
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The single most effective treatment in the beekeeping calendar.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A broodless winter colony is the only time varroa mites have nowhere to hide from treatment. A single oxalic acid application in December or January, when all the mites are on adult bees, kills over 90% of the varroa load. Miss the window and the mites come out of winter with a head start on the new season.
